title: What do Japanese fans think of Shinji
display_title: What do Japanese fans think of Shinji
wikitext:
[]: What do Japanese fans think of Shinji?             Quote Post by GAP » Wed May 21, 2014 3:35 pm While the hate for Shinji is a lot mroe prevalent in the West, I want to know why Japanese fans think of Shinji's character as a critique on otaku culture?

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed May 21, 2014 5:16 pm Because Anno sort of said it? Although the degree of 'critique' often gets wildly blown out of proportion. Shinji is not particularly close to being a literal Otaku or basement dweller material; Too 'correct' for that. If the events of the show had never happened, he'd probably have become some soulless office drone. Pays taxes, does little else, that sort of peripheral existence. And in any case, he's not thirty and in someone's basement; He's pretty much the appropiate age to still need to learn and be learning the lessons he gets exposed to over the course of the show. But the ocassional Japanese forum member has remarked that he's a lot closer to an 'everyman' depiction for over there, although... the behavior/issues of socially awkward teens do not vary that much across the globe, to someone who had similar experienes, he's pretty much fully relatable, regardless of where in the world they are sitting, at least as long as we're talking of industrialized nation living conditions. I'd look for the reasns of the really intense hate in American culture, not to find reasons for the lack thereof in the audience he was intended for.

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Wed May 21, 2014 5:40 pm Kendrix wrote: I'd look for the reasns of the really intense hate in American culture, not to find reasons for the lack thereof in the audience he was intended for.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Wed May 21, 2014 5:47 pm It might depend on how a person, in part influenced by their cultural context, react to/deal with mirrors. Something that also turns up in discussions often is ideas of what someone owes themselves and the world; When you have a culture that's all about, "If you're unfortunate it's probably your own fault; Suck it up and don't bother the rest of us.", they're not going to react favorably to a person like him. And of course, Japan also has its own cultural pecuiarities about the roles of the individual and their contributions that might be had for outsiders, including me, to quantify in a non-formulaic way. On the other hand, it's possible that people just don't like them; No need to infer freudian implications about them just because they don't share our personal taste.

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Wed May 21, 2014 9:46 pm But the ocassional Japanese forum member has remarked that he's a lot closer to an 'everyman' depiction for over there, although... the behavior/issues of socially awkward teens do not vary that much across the globe, to someone who had similar experienes, he's pretty much fully relatable, regardless of where in the world they are sitting, at least as long as we're talking of industrialized nation living conditions. I'd look for the reasns of the really intense hate in American culture, not to find reasons for the lack thereof in the audience he was intended for.

[]:             Quote Post by Wanderlust » Wed May 21, 2014 10:24 pm Shinji seems popular enough in Japan. He was the #1 most popular male character in a 1997 Animage poll, and in a Newtype poll in 2010 he was voted as the most popular male character from the 1990s. Though the latter might be influenced by Rebuild I suppose, as he usually pops up in their polls after a new film comes out. It's probably worth mentioning though that in both cases Kaworu came in second, so in all probability a good portion of Shinji's fans in Japan are fujoshi who like to ship him with Kaworu. Still, there's no denying the impact Shinji had on the industry. Before he came around, the protagonists of mecha shows tended to be all gung-ho and hot-blooded, but post-Shinji more and more sensitive and reluctant heroes started popping up. As for why he's hated in the west, I simply chalk it up to the fact that most people in the west don't like seeing flawed characters with low self-esteem portrayed in fiction. They prefer more confident and composed traditional heroes that they can look up to. Hell, they don't even have to be heroes, lots of people will cheer for a confident asshole bad guy over a shy and sensitive good guy.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu May 22, 2014 6:08 am Wanderlust wrote: As for why he's hated in the west, I simply chalk it up to the fact that most people in the west don't like seeing flawed characters with low self-esteem portrayed in fiction. They prefer more confident and composed traditional heroes that they can look up to. Hell, they don't even have to be heroes, lots of people will cheer for a confident asshole bad guy over a shy and sensitive good guy.

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Thu May 22, 2014 8:09 am ^ Very good post Mari, I'd just like to remark, that some Western people also mentioned, that in Q Shinji is finally 100% relatable. Even if they didn't like the movie, the one thing ok with it was seemingly, that you could finally understand Shinji's whining.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu May 22, 2014 9:25 am "The west" isn't a homogenous block, tho. There is a lot of stuff west of Japan. Another factor that might or might not influence the perception of it is where someone stands on a scale of intent vs results as they feature in in judging the morality someone's actions, although the 'intent' part also overlaps/intersects with someone's view on obligation (See, for example, the whole 'selfishness' debate) --- the one thing ok with it was seemingly, that you could finally understand Shinji's whining.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu May 22, 2014 4:24 pm robersora wrote: ^ Very good post Mari, I'd just like to remark, that some Western people also mentioned, that in Q Shinji is finally 100% relatable. Even if they didn't like the movie, the one thing ok with it was seemingly, that you could finally understand Shinji's whining.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu May 22, 2014 4:44 pm I don't want to derail this since there's already a why hate Shinji thread, but the assessment everyone gives that Shinji's lack of confidence is the reason behind the Western audience's dislike of the character is wrong. Conflicted characters who lack confidence in themselves and are unsure about things are so common I can't believe that anyone would honestly say that's why he's unpopular over here. Every hero has conflict or you can't tell a story about them. One of the most popular hero tropes in Western fiction is the reluctant hero. Shinji is unpopular in the west because he's passive. He doesn't do things, things happen to him. The basic rules of storytelling dictate that this makes him a bad character and a poor storytelling device. He's also unpopular because he's a fairly realistic exemplar of what a real thirteen year old would do in his situation. If he was exactly the same but he was a more proactive protagonist who caused things to happen and solved some of his own problems forcefully, he'd be much more popular. The other thing that makes him unpopular in the West isn't really him, it's the face saving/shame culture that he comes from. The expectations placed on him chafe a Western audience who does not grasp the nuance of why he can't just tell his dad to fuck off or confront the authority figures around him.

[]:             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Thu May 22, 2014 4:52 pm People in the west are assholes lol. I don't know what they expect from a 14 year old. Also a lot of people think of the series as pretenious, so having this guy as the protagonist of a robot show is probably pretentious too...or something. They fail to understand that Ikari Shinji is love and life.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Thu May 22, 2014 4:59 pm ^^ To put it simply, Shinji as a character in a story... is boring. Something like this?

[]:             Quote Post by kuribo-04 » Thu May 22, 2014 5:11 pm Isn't a character that is not a stereotype interesting and fun? I am entertained by Shinji. But I guess that's opinion.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Thu May 22, 2014 5:11 pm Stillborn wrote: ^^ To put it simply, Shinji as a character in a story... is boring. Something like this?

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu May 22, 2014 6:51 pm Not boring, wrong. Also I said basic rules. When you're an auteur they no longer apply. Rules were meant to be broken, and suggestions were meant to be ignored.

[]:             Quote Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Thu May 22, 2014 10:49 pm My theory is the western hate stems from western societies inability to rationalize a vulnerable and passive male hero. A lot of Shinjis actions are things we relate to a female protagonist. Westerners reject this idea since it challenges our perceptions of gender roles.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Thu May 22, 2014 11:03 pm Shinji is not passive. I made this list elsewhere but Shinji: 1. Reversed his initial refusal to pilot for Rei's sake 2. Went into a berserk fury to protect Toji and Kensuke even after the former punched him 3. Chose to fire the positron gun even though Rei offered to take his place 4. Dove into an active volcano to save Asuka without protective equipment 5. Pushed Asuka out of the way and was swallowed into a black hole 6. Was willing to die himself to uphold his moral stance and refuse to harm Toji 7. Abandoned a principled moral stance to stop Zeruel 8. Wanted to go into battle against Arael despite the clear risk of suffering the same, obviously terrible fate Asuka was experiencing 9. Was again willing to sacrifice himself for Rei He's not a passive character, or a weak character, or a spineless character, but he gets called those things all the time. Why? Because he's a victim of forces beyond his control, and Western audiences want to see a person like Shinji triumph over adversity. They don't want to see him victimized over and over again. The hate on Shinji in the west comes from blaming him for the narrative he's a part of. They don't like the idea that failure is possible, that you won't always be smooth with the girl, that even stoic willingness to sacrifice one's self unto death sometimes isn't enough and there's just nothing we can do to win in a no-win situation. So they blame Shinji, because by blaming him they are implying they would do better, they would solve the problem, they would save the girl and beat the monsters. Before someone points it out, yes, I called him passive in my prior post. I chose the wrong word.

[]:             Quote Post by Ray » Thu May 22, 2014 11:24 pm I had a whole thread on the Rebuild forum discussing the whole, Grimdark inevitable sad end that Shinji couldn't prevent. It's an Issue of cultural differences more than anything else. Japan and Asian Cultures tend to believe things are inevitable (speaking broadly), and that things are beyond ones control. Americans like myself prefer believing in the free will to control our fate.

[]:             Quote Post by GAP » Fri May 23, 2014 2:36 am Shinji isn't a character who going to be a badass action hero but the point of the TV series is that he tried and he does try to make an effort. That is not to say that all his problems will go away but he learns that he needs to accept himself. Shinji is a classic tragic her but unlike most of them, it is implied that things will get better.
